data.content:
Use `dotnet watch test` — it monitors your files and re-runs tests automatically on save:

```
dotnet watch test --filter "RemoveStock_ExceedsAvailable_ThrowsException"
```

The `--filter` flag scopes it to just your failing test for faster feedback. Let me start it so you can see it in action:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Run dotnet watch test with filter

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Run dotnet watch test with filter

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to set up hot reload for their test project. Since they're using .NET 10 with Microsoft.Testing.Platform (as specified in global.json), they can use `dotnet test --hotreload` or `dotnet watch test`.

Let me check what's available. With .NET 10 and Microsoft.Testing.Platform, `dotnet watch` with tests should work. Let me suggest `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically when files change.

Actually, looking more carefully, with the Microsoft.Testing.Platform runner, `dotnet watch test` should work, but there's also the newer `dotnet test --hotreload` option in newer .NET versions. Let me just suggest the standard `dotnet watch test` approach which works well.


Let me test this to make sure it actually works.

data.reasoningText:
The issue is that `global.json` specifies `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` as the test runner, but the project isn't configured to use it. I need to add `<TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>` to the project file, or use the `--enable-microsoft-testing-platform` property.

Actually, let me check - the project is using MSTest 3.8.0 and the global.json says to use Microsoft.Testing.Platform. The project needs `<OutputType>Exe</OutputType>` and `<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>` to use the Microsoft.Testing.Platform runner. 

With MSTest 3.x, I can either enable the runner directly with those properties, or configure `dotnet test` integration with the testing platform by setting the appropriate support flag.
